entry.title: Brewistas vs. Broistas: 7 Brew applies for another local location as it takes on Dutch Bros.
Gas station + BBQ = best friends: Local barbecue joint expands to Meridian
Redneck sodas and hillbilly rounds: New C-Store planned for Star
Fast-casual burger chain adding another Treasure Valley location
Sushi Sushi lives up to its name with second Sushi Sushi
Italian ice, custard, and ‘gelata’ coming to Treasure Valley
Star Mayor says legislature is ‘failing’ local communities before vote on big impact fee hike
Star is struggling to pay for police and fire services. A new fee helps with costs, but is it legal?
Crowd ousts canal board over opposition to recycled water project. Board pres. calls it ‘lynch mob mentality’
‘Call it the DMZ’: Canyon Co. votes on what to do with disputed area between Star, Nampa
‘One of the best pizzas in town’: Well-known Boise restaurant owners buy pizza truck after closing eatery
Cascade ‘community landmark’ listed for sale
‘Its own thing’: Owners of Idaho’s oldest bar branch out with purchase of Boise mainstay
Scent of Home: Kuna-based company crafts non-toxic soy candles for every home
The Edit Idaho: New consignment boutique opens in Caldwell
New coffee shop to debut in Middleton
New indoor golf training facility opening in Meridian
‘Fun, glam shopping experience’: New women’s clothing and accessories boutique opening in Caldwell
‘Personalized little space’: Weeklee Closet brings rotating thrift booths to Nampa
‘Hat for every head’: Custom hat maker opens storefront in downtown Boise
‘One of the best pizzas in town’: Well-known Boise restaurant owners buy pizza truck after closing eatery
Cascade ‘community landmark’ listed for sale
‘Its own thing’: Owners of Idaho’s oldest bar branch out with purchase of Boise mainstay
New coffee shop to debut in Middleton
What’s next at Bogus Basin: Leaders look at expansion & improvement plans
Circus training facility finally lands a home in the Treasure Valley all to themselves 🎪
Circus training facility finally lands a home in the Treasure Valley all to themselves 🎪
Outside in: Wahooz plans new indoor kart racing, Pinz bar remodel
Large empty space in Boise mall set for new ‘high-energy’ tenant from Spokane
No ocean needed: Two supersized sandboxes set to open
Brewistas vs. Broistas: 7 Brew applies for another local location as it takes on Dutch Bros.
entry.summary: They’re coming to the Village at Meridian and eyeing a location in an old car wash. Now, 7 Brew is looking at a new coffee drive-thru along State St. As BoiseDev has reported, fast-growing Dutch Bros ‘look-a-like’ 7 Brew is plotting expansion to the Boise area. Core States Group applied on behalf of 7 Brew to convert a building at 6940 W. State St. in Boise into a new drive-thru location for the chain. The building is currently leased to Papa Murphy’s Take ‘N’ Bake Pizza and Boise Fry Co. in the Northgate Shopping Center. A leasing flier shows the Papa Murphy’s space is currently available for lease. New owners took over Northgate last year, as BoiseDev reported. The small drive-thru lane used by Papa Murphy’s would remain, with queuing space for six cars. Core States says runners would be used to take orders. The application with the city is preliminary and could change or not move forward. The City of Boise’s zoning code severely limits the number of new drive-thrus that can be built, and places significant restrictions on where they can be added and how they can look. But existing drive-thru facilities like the one here are largely grandfathered in under older building codes. 7 Brew’s menu includes items like the Cinnamon Roll (a brown sugar, cinnamon, and white chocolate breve), the Brunette (a mocha with hazelnut and caramel), and the Cookie Butter (a breve with toasted marshmallows, hazelnut, and white chocolate). A line of energy drinks includes offerings like peach, raspberry, and even a Pixie Stick flavor that features a blend of almond, orange, and pomegranate. While Dutch Bros. centers its marketing around adjectives like happiness, prosperity, and optimism, 7 Brew says it “cultivate(s) kindness in every cup.” Dutch Bros. calls its workers Broistas, while 7 Brew calls them Brewistas. The WSJ says 7 Brew, which is backed by private equity firms, is a “Dutch Bros. look-alike.” It is the fourth-fastest-growing chain in terms of market share growth between 2019 and 2024, behind Dutch, Starbucks, and Scooters’ Coffee. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. 7 Brew has more than 730 drive-thru locations across 38 states, with over 100 in Texas. 7 Brew had one stand in Rogers, Arkansas, in 2017, and just 19 by 2019. It has opened new outlets at a clip of more than 100 per year.

entry.title: Gas station + BBQ = best friends: Local barbecue joint expands to Meridian
entry.summary: Brisket, pulled pork, and baby back ribs will soon be available in North Meridian. Neighbor Tim’s BBQ is planning a new location at 1410 W. McMillan Rd. It is taking over the space next to the Stinker’s Store that previously housed Gandolfo’s. “I think it’s just great. McMillan and Linder is full of houses. Rocky Mountain High School is just right down the street. There’s all sorts of other eateries there,” said Neighbor Tim’s BBQ owner Tim Goebel. “Then being attached to the Stinker gas station, we can obviously partner up with that, where they’re bringing in guests for us and we’re bringing in guests for them.” There will be a walk-through between Stinker’s and Neighbor Tims, and Goebel is excited to be connected with the gas station. “I’ve always felt like a gas station and a barbecue spot are like best friends, you know what I mean,” he said. “So I’ve always had this dream of like being attached with a gas station… I just think it’s awesome.” Goebel said the menu will be consistent with the other two locations, featuring a range of barbecued and smoked meats, including brisket, pulled pork, German sausage, baby back ribs, and BBQ chicken. There are also several classic barbecue sides like mac and cheese, coleslaw, baked beans, and potato salad. The new location will feature indoor and outdoor seating, along with a drive-thru. Neighbor Tim’s is celebrating five years in business. BoiseDev reported when Neighbor Tim’s opened its first location at 7802 W. Goddard Rd. in Boise in 2021. It opened its second location in The Warehouse in 2022 as one of the original residents. Goebel said he is planning minimal updates to the new space and expects to open by mid-September. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Goebel is also still on the hunt for a new location for Tim’s Burgers, a concept he opened at The Boardwalk in early 2025 and closed abruptly in October. Tim’s Burgers and Bao Boi, both former tenants at The Boardwalk, are currently in the midst of lawsuits against Crispy Investments, their landlord, alleging lease issues and defamation, as BoiseDev reported. Goebel cited exclusives as the main challenge in finding a new location. “We’re still marinating on a place for that. The difficulty I’m having is that the majority of corners that are available for me to put a burger spot, have a burger spot already in that corner, which is fine. I’ll compete, that doesn’t matter to me,” he said. “It’s more they have exclusives on shopping centers, so I can’t come in because there’s another burger joint that has the exclusive on the shopping center.” He is still optimistic and hopes to find the right spot for Tim’s Burgers. “I still have aspirations to do that. It was a great concept and a great product. I hear praise about it all the time,” Goebel said. “… We’re still on the hunt for the best corner we can find.”

entry.title: Fast-casual burger chain adding another Treasure Valley location
entry.summary: A well-known burger chain is set to open another location in the Treasure Valley next year. Habit Burger & Grill is a fast-casual chain restaurant founded in California. There are over 370 locations in the United States, and the chain is best known for its “Charburgers”. The new location of Habit Burger is moving into the previous KeyBank location at 3314 E Chinden Blvd in Eagle. Director of Asset Management at Alturas, Michael Bick, grew up in Southern California and moved to Boise in 2018 for school. After graduating and landing an internship, he got a full-time position at Alturas and has been making connections in the community ever since. Bick and his team have leasing brokers at Eagle Marketplace where Habit Burger is going, and this connection is how the process for opening a franchise came to be. Bick and the team at Eagle Marketplace came to an agreement on leasing terms, and after a few months of negotiating, the process is moving along. The new location is offering a drive-through option in addition to dining room seating. Parking is located on the side and rear of the building. It will be the third Habit Burger location in the Treasure Valley, with current locations in Nampa and Meridian. In February 2025, BoiseDev reported on two additional planned Habit Burger locations: one on Ten Mile Rd. in Meridian and another on Milwaukee St. in Boise. The Ten Mile location is moving forward and is currently under construction in the Ten Mile Crossing development, which also houses Sheels. The planning permit for the Milwaukee location has expired. Bick said even though this location is close in proximity to the Meridian location, “I feel like demographically, it’s split a little bit, just from a traffic perspective.” There is no set opening date for Habit Burger Eagle, however Bick said they expect to be open by spring of 2027.

entry.title: Sushi Sushi lives up to its name with second Sushi Sushi
entry.summary: Another location of an established sushi restaurant in Nampa is now open for business. Sushi Sushi is a casual sushi and Chinese food restaurant that has been located at 16734 N Marketplace Blvd in Nampa for over eight years. A second location is now open in Meridian at 6700 N. Linder Rd, next to the UPS store. Manager of Sushi Sushi, Stacy Lin, is originally from New York City but moved to Idaho over six years ago and started her journey with Sushi Sushi not long after. The new Meridian location of Sushi Sushi is almost identical to Nampa’s; however, Lin said one addition for the Meridian location is the use of DoorDash for online ordering. Sushi Sushi is open seven days a week, and Lin said that, in honor of Sushi Sushi Meridian’s grand opening on Monday, June 22, 2026, everyone will be given a discount for supporting the opening of the new location. Lin said her main goal for this expansion is to “Serve the community. So, we’re doing good business, and customers can find a good place to dine and to take (food) to go too. This is our goal, and to make it a win-win for our business and the Meridian community.”

entry.title: What’s next at Bogus Basin: Leaders look at expansion & improvement plans
entry.summary: Skiers, snowboarders, and mountain bikers rejoice: Bogus Basin has some new projects in the works. Bogus Basin Director of Mountain Operations Nate Shake said that Bogus Basin recently received approval for four new projects at the recreation area — two new alpine trails, a chairlift realignment, and two mountain bike trail extensions. Because the recreation area operates on U.S. Forest Service land with a special use permit, that agency had to sign off on upgrades and changes to the organization’s land use within that special use permit boundary, Shake said. Shake said that this summer, crews will clear and grade two new groomed runs in Pine Creek. Pine Creek sits on the back side of Shafer Butte. The organization also received approval to realign the Showcase chair lift up the south face of Deer Point Mountain to Cabin Traverse. That change will serve the back side of the mountain, Shake said. He estimated that the work could be done in the next 2-5 years, depending on snowfall in the next several winters, which tends to drive revenue that the organization can put toward new projects. The 2025-2026 winter season brought an unusually low snowpack to many of Idaho’s watersheds, as BoiseDev reported. On April 1, the point at which basins transition from peak annual snowpack to spring melt-off, Bogus Basin sat at 62% of normal snow water equivalent, a measurement of water storage, BoiseDev reported. “If we go into another winter similar to this one, it slows our ability to pay for these improvements,” Shake said. The next projects in the works are two mountain bike trail extensions, Shake said. The first would extend Deer Point Trail to Around the Mountain Trail. The second would extend Elk Meadows Trail to the top of the Morning Star Express chair lift. Connecting Elk Meadows to the top of the lift completes Elk Meadows as a single track trail all the way around Shafer’s Butte, he said. So how does Bogus Basin decide which projects to move forward with? We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Shake said that Bogus Basin developed its first master plan in 1996, and created a new one in 2016. Master plans tend to outline an organization’s plans for development over a set period of time, such as ten years. He said that the organization is currently operating off of its 2016 master plan. Bogus Basin leadership used the organization’s strategic business plan to create the master plan, with each proposed project corresponding to a revenue increase, he said. Since 2016, Bogus Basin has spent $80 million on projects, all paid for without the organization taking on debt, Shake said. One project included installing the mountain’s original snowmaking pond, and installing snowmaking infrastructure on about 100 acres, Shake said. The plan was amended in 2023 to allow for several improvements, including expanding night skiing terrain on the Superior and Triumph runs, the installation of the Coach’s chairlift, doubling the length of Coach’s Corner run, and adding snowmaking devices and night lighting. Shake said that the organization might amend the plan one more time, likely in 2026, before developing a new master plan. An amendment would include expanding the recreation area’s operating terrain, including adding new lifts and trails outside of the recreation area’s current permitted area with the Forest Service, he said. Following that, it could take two to five years to develop and put a new master plan in place, according to Shake. As leaders contemplate changes, they are also thinking of infrastructure improvements, like parking and lodges, to accommodate additional visitors. Pioneer Lodge underwent a $5 million remodel, and next winter, visitors will be able to enjoy a new 10,000 sq. ft. food, beverage, and event facility with capacity for 250 people near the Pioneer base area, Shake said. BoiseDev shared details about the new facility earlier this year.

entry.title: Circus training facility finally lands a home in the Treasure Valley all to themselves 🎪
entry.summary: There is an official location for a circus training center just around the corner from the Boise Costco. Circus Vision is moving into a warehouse located at 2007 S. Saturn Way in Boise and is expected to open this spring. Samantha Buckmier, owner and director of Circus Vision, started doing aerial arts in Oregon when she was 18 years old. Though she started as a dancer, after being taught aerial, she quickly became obsessed and started training more seriously. Buckmier ended up moving to different locations out west to start touring and training with special programs, but she realized the serious atmosphere of aerial and flying was not for her. That’s when she decided to move back to Boise and start her own business for circus act training and create a positive atmosphere and supportive environment for the circus community. Buckmier said, “Circus vision is an aerial arts school, a flying trapeze school, and a production company. So, within our aerial school, we teach adult and youth classes, and we also have youth and adult competition teams.” A few interesting things that Buckmier said “happened by accident” are that Circus Vision has an all-female staff, which is rare in the circus industry, and her entire staff is certified Safe Sport Instructors and either involved in healthcare or teaching. Buckmier has been looking for a new and improved home for Circus Vision for about three years now, and the process has not been easy. The biggest struggle has been finding a building that has high enough ceilings because “We need height to make it (training) feasible,” Buckmier said. “Ideally, we wanted somewhere with 26 feet for aerial, but that doesn’t really exist in Idaho,” Buckmier said. “So I’ve been looking for three years, and the building that we just found is about 17 feet, which is great because we can still do a lot of things, and it is still the highest in Idaho.” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Circus Vision has been subleasing from other facilities to train and is currently training at Tumble Time Gymnastics on Cloverdale Road in Boise. “So we’ve been there since last April, and we’ll be there until we get to move into our new space,” Buckmier said. The new location for Circus Vision is a large warehouse that is being converted into an aerial studio. In addition to the aerial practice areas, Buckmier said, “the back part of the warehouse has fitness equipment, and we offer a fitness membership.” This is an option for people who aren’t really into aerial, but still enjoy being around the circus community. Buckmier said she hopes to have the entire training facility open by the third week of May and offering training seven days a week. The main goal of Circus Vision is to be a place of inclusivity and positivity in the aerial and circus community. “We modify classes to make sure everyone feels good about themselves before they leave our class,” Buckmier said. “Now that we’ll be in our space, it gives us a lot of opportunities,” Buckmier said. “We can be permanent, grow our program, and also this gives us the opportunity to start producing shows.”

entry.title: Circus training facility finally lands a home in the Treasure Valley all to themselves 🎪
entry.summary: There is an official location for a circus training center just around the corner from the Boise Costco. Circus Vision is moving into a warehouse located at 2007 S. Saturn Way in Boise and is expected to open this spring. Samantha Buckmier, owner and director of Circus Vision, started doing aerial arts in Oregon when she was 18 years old. Though she started as a dancer, after being taught aerial, she quickly became obsessed and started training more seriously. Buckmier ended up moving to different locations out west to start touring and training with special programs, but she realized the serious atmosphere of aerial and flying was not for her. That’s when she decided to move back to Boise and start her own business for circus act training and create a positive atmosphere and supportive environment for the circus community. Buckmier said, “Circus vision is an aerial arts school, a flying trapeze school, and a production company. So, within our aerial school, we teach adult and youth classes, and we also have youth and adult competition teams.” A few interesting things that Buckmier said “happened by accident” are that Circus Vision has an all-female staff, which is rare in the circus industry, and her entire staff is certified Safe Sport Instructors and either involved in healthcare or teaching. Buckmier has been looking for a new and improved home for Circus Vision for about three years now, and the process has not been easy. The biggest struggle has been finding a building that has high enough ceilings because “We need height to make it (training) feasible,” Buckmier said. “Ideally, we wanted somewhere with 26 feet for aerial, but that doesn’t really exist in Idaho,” Buckmier said. “So I’ve been looking for three years, and the building that we just found is about 17 feet, which is great because we can still do a lot of things, and it is still the highest in Idaho.” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Circus Vision has been subleasing from other facilities to train and is currently training at Tumble Time Gymnastics on Cloverdale Road in Boise. “So we’ve been there since last April, and we’ll be there until we get to move into our new space,” Buckmier said. The new location for Circus Vision is a large warehouse that is being converted into an aerial studio. In addition to the aerial practice areas, Buckmier said, “the back part of the warehouse has fitness equipment, and we offer a fitness membership.” This is an option for people who aren’t really into aerial, but still enjoy being around the circus community. Buckmier said she hopes to have the entire training facility open by the third week of May and offering training seven days a week. The main goal of Circus Vision is to be a place of inclusivity and positivity in the aerial and circus community. “We modify classes to make sure everyone feels good about themselves before they leave our class,” Buckmier said. “Now that we’ll be in our space, it gives us a lot of opportunities,” Buckmier said. “We can be permanent, grow our program, and also this gives us the opportunity to start producing shows.”

entry.title: Outside in: Wahooz plans new indoor kart racing, Pinz bar remodel
entry.summary: A local family recreation center is planning one of the largest expansions in its 27-year history. Wahooz Family Fun Zone plans to begin construction this July on a new, 52,000 sq. ft. indoor electric karting facility, dubbed Nitroz High Speed Racing, according to a news release from the company. The new facility will feature two tracks with elevation changes and dramatic, LED lighting. The 72-kart fleet can reach speeds of 30-40 mph. A third “junior track will ensure a safe, exciting introduction to karting for younger drivers,” the release said. “Performance-style kart racing has been growing globally. Now, Wahooz is bringing that elevated experience to Idaho,” Wahooz CEO Pat Morandi said. The expansion also includes a 3,800 sq. ft. event space for corporate gatherings and birthday parties, the release said. Planning documents indicate the event space will be branded Lightspeed Meeting Rooms. Black Mor Inc., which owns Wahooz and Roaring Springs, applied with the City of Meridian to expand the property for Wahooz, and submitted materials for design review of the expansion. The new facility is being built where the company’s slick track currently exists, and the release said that the batting cages have already been removed to make way for Nitroz. The larger outdoor road track visible from Interstate 84 will remain. Black Mor also applied to revamp the Pinz Bar, located in another part of the Wahooz building. The application indicates a $355,327 remodel, including demolition of the current bar and build-out of a new bar layout. Black Mor, which opened Roaring Springs in 1999, purchased Boondocks Fun Center in 2010, and renamed it Wahooz. Under Black Mor’s ownership, Wahooz has expanded the facility numerous times, adding the Pinz indoor bowling center, Galaxy Eventer Center and a large expansion to Boondocks’ core play areas. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. BoiseDev’s Don Day contributed reporting.

entry.title: Large empty space in Boise mall set for new ‘high-energy’ tenant from Spokane
entry.summary: Forever 21 is but a memory, but the large empty space at Boise Towne Square could soon see activity again. The fast-fashion retailer closed last year, leaving 16,000 square feet of space on the mall’s upper level vacant. It led to a spike in empty stores that put the mall over the 20% vacancy mark, as BoiseDev has reported. But permits filed with the City of Boise indicate a large indoor kids’ play zone called We Play could be coming soon. A permit valued at $595,000 would revamp the former clothing store into a spot for kids to play, along with new restrooms and partitions, according to the filing with the city. We Play has an existing location in the Spokane Valley Mall, featuring a variety of attractions for kids aged six months to 12 years. It also offers birthday party packages, snacks, and drinks. On Facebook, owners say they plan to open by early fall. “We Play Boise is a Premier Indoor Playground designed for the entire family to connect, explore, and make unforgettable memories — all in a clean, safe, high-energy environment where imagination takes center stage,” they wrote. Attractions planned for Boise include a sports arena, trampolines, a junior ninja warrior course, a volcano slide, and more. Boise Towne Square has seen an increasing number of experiential businesses join the usual mix of stores and restaurants. Another mall space is occupied by a Glowgolf indoor mini-golf business, but more than six months after moving in, it has not yet opened. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Across Milwaukee Street, another indoor entertainment venue is planned, with the Slick City indoor slide zone project receiving administrative approval from the City of Boise earlier this week.

entry.title: No ocean needed: Two supersized sandboxes set to open
entry.summary: Families in the Treasure Valley will soon have two new places to dig, climb, and play, as a pair of oversized sandbox-style playgrounds roll out this spring. Sandbox by Hill House opened its first sandbox location in Meridian on March 9th and will be opening a second location in Garden City later this spring. “Our Meridian location is open, airy, and centered around an expansive sand play area,” Sandbox by Hill House’s owners said. “The soon-to-open Garden City location offers immersive play, unique gross-motor integration, and thoughtful programming.” Both locations are designed for kids ages 0-12. “Our Meridian Sandbox offers a wide, open space designed for digging, building, experimenting, and letting imagination take shape,” the Sandbox’s owners said. “Kids move naturally between toys, tools, and materials — creating their own games as they go.” The Meridian Sandbox spans more than 14,000 square feet and features 600 tons of sand sourced locally from Emmett, Idaho. The Garden City Sandbox will cover more than 12,000 square feet and include three themed play areas, each filled with 80 tons of sand from the Bahamas. “Sandbox takes cleanliness seriously, especially in a space designed for children. Our team cleans toys and carefully grooms the sand every day to keep the environment fresh and inviting,” Sandbox’s owners said. “It’s all part of creating a space where families can relax and focus on play.” Admission is $5 for infants ages 7 to 12 months and ranges from $15 for children ages 1 to 12 in Meridian to $20 at the upcoming Garden City sandbox, with free entry for adults. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. The Meridian Sandbox is located at 1252 E. Piper Ct. in Idaho Beach. Meridian, ID 83642, and is open Monday-Friday 9 a.m. to 1 p.m., and Saturdays 2 p.m. to 7 p.m. Construction on the Garden City location at 6843 N Strawberry Glenn Rd. is underway and is set to open in May.
